notes.anchor: written in a set of notes and given to Eros
notes.note: Codicilli were small wax tablets used for memoranda and short messages, here carrying Cicero's detailed reply. Eros was an accountant-secretary who handled Cicero's affairs.

notes.anchor: on the matter of young Cicero
notes.note: Cicero's son Marcus, then about eighteen, who wanted to go to Caesar's army in Spain and to receive a larger allowance. His father feared the political censure of sending his son to fight on the Caesarian side after laying down arms himself.

notes.anchor: what the flamen Lentulus gives his son
notes.note: Cicero benchmarks his son's allowance against what other fathers settle on their sons, here a Cornelius Lentulus holding one of the flaminates (state priesthoods). The particular flamen cannot be securely identified, nor can the "Publilius" cited alongside him.

notes.anchor: outdone in intimacy and in every kind of favour by his cousin
notes.note: The cousin is young Quintus Cicero, the nephew, already in close standing with Caesar; Latin frater here means cousin. Cicero fears his own son would resent being eclipsed by him.
